volumeTitle: The Merchant. The Braggart Soldier. The Ghost. The Persian
label: The Merchant (Section 1.1.1 - Section 1.2.154_155)

sections.label: Section 1.1.1
sections.original: Duas res simul nunc agere decretumst mihi :
sections.translation: Two things have I now resolved to do at the same time ; both the subject and my own amours will I disclose . I am not doing like as I have seen other lovers do in Comedies , who relate their woes either to the night or to the day ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.5
sections.original: aut soli aut lunae miserias narrant suas :
sections.translation: or to the Sun or to the Moon ; who , faith , I don ’ t suppose pay much regard to the complaints of mortals , either what they wish or what they don ’ t wish . To yourselves in preference will I now relate my woes . In the Greek this Play is called the Emporos [*] of Philemon ;

sections.label: Section 1.1.10
sections.original: eadem Latine Mercator Macci Titi .
sections.translation: the same in the Latin is the Mercator of Marcus Accius . My father sent me hence to trade at Rhodes . Two years have now passed since I left home . There I began to love a fair one of remarkable beauty . But how I was captivated by her , I ’ ll tell you , if you ’ ll lend ear ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.15
sections.original: atque advortendum ad animum adest benignitas .
sections.translation: and if you ’ ll have the kindness to give your attention to this . And yet in this , but little have I followed the method of our forefathers in my own person , and on the spot as a tell-tale [*] of my own amours am I represented before you . But all these failings are wont to attend on love — care , trouble , and refinement overmuch .

sections.label: Section 1.1.20
sections.original: haec non modo illum quí amat , sed quemque attigit
sections.translation: Not only him who loves , but every one to whom this latter fault extends , him with a great and weighty evil does it affect ; nor by my troth , in fact , does any one aim at refinement , beyond what his means allow of , without heavy disaster . But to love as well are these evils incident , which I have not as yet recounted —

sections.label: Section 1.1.25
sections.original: insomnia , aerumna , error , terror et fuga ,
sections.translation: sleeplessness , a troubled mind , confusion , terror , and apprehension , trifling , and folly even , rashness too , thoughtlessness , foolhardy impudence , wantonness , lust , and malevolence ; covetousness is inherent as well , idleness , injustice ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.30
sections.original: inopia , contumelia et dispendium ,
sections.translation: want , contumely and wastefulness , talkativeness or moody silence . This latter is the fact , because things which relate not to the purpose , nor are of utility , the same does the lover give utterance to full oft at an unseasonable moment ; and yet again , this moody silence for this reason do I commend ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.35
sections.original: quia nullus umquam amator adeost callide
sections.translation: because no lover is ever so skilled in eloquence , as to be enabled to give utterance to that which is for his own interest . You , then , must not be offended with myself for my babbling ; Venus bestowed it upon me on that same day on which this passion . To that point am I resolved to return , that what I commenced upon I may disclose .

sections.label: Section 1.1.40
sections.original: principio ut ex ephebis aetate exii
sections.translation: In the first place , when in life I had passed from my boyish days , and my disposition was weaned from childish pursuits , I began distractedly to love a Courtesan in this place . Forthwith , unknown to my father , my means went to be wasted upon her ; an exacting Procurer , the owner of this damsel ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.45
sections.original: vi summa ut quicque poterat rapiebat domum .
sections.translation: by every method that he could , grasped everything into his own possession [*] . Night and day my father censured me for this ; represented the perfidy , the wickedness of Procurers ; how that his own property was being forcibly rent in pieces , while that of this Procurer was increasing ; this too in the loudest tones ; sometimes muttering to himself ;

sections.label: Section 1.1.50
sections.original: abnuere , negitare adeo me natum suom .
sections.translation: refusing to speak to me ; even denying that I was his child ; crying aloud through all the city and proclaiming that all should withhold from trusting me when desiring to borrow ; that love had allured many a one to ruin ; that I , passing all bounds , regardless of decency , and acting wrongfully ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.55
sections.original: trahere , exhaurire me quod quirem ab se domo ;
sections.translation: laid hands upon and tore whatever I could from him at home ; that ’ twas a most vile system that those choice possessions which he , by enduring every hardship , had acquired , should all be squandered away and parted with through the violence of my desire . That now for so many years he had supported myself , a reproach to him ;

sections.label: Section 1.1.60
sections.original: quod nisi puderet , ne luberet vivere .
sections.translation: that were I not ashamed , I ought not to desire to live . That he himself , at the very moment after he had passed his boyish days , did not , like me , devote his attention to love or indolence in slothfulness , nor , indeed , had he the control of himself , so very strictly by his father was he held in check ;

sections.label: Section 1.1.65
sections.original: multo opere immundo rustico se exercitum ,
sections.translation: that in the various sordid pursuits of the country he was employed , and that only every fifth year even was he then enabled to visit the city , and that immediately after he had had a sight of the Festival [*] , back again instantly into the country was he wont to be driven by his father . That there by far the most of all the household

sections.label: Section 1.1.70
sections.original: laboravisse , quom haec pater sibi diceret :
sections.translation: did he toil , while thus his father would say to him : For yourself you are ploughing , for yourself you harrow , for yourself you sow , for your own self too do you reap ; for yourself , in fine , will this labour be productive of happiness . That after life had left his father ’ s body , he had sold the farm , and with that money

sections.label: Section 1.1.75
sections.original: navem , metretas quae trecentas tolleret ,
sections.translation: had bought for himself a bark of fifteen tons [*] , and with the same had transported merchandize to every quarter , even until he had acquired the property which he then possessed . That I ought to do the same , if I would be as it behoved me to be .

sections.label: Section 1.1.79_80
sections.original: ego mé ubi invisum meo patri esse intellego
sections.translation: I , when I found that I was disliked by my father and was an object of hate to him whom I was bound to please , distracted and in love as I was , resolutely made up my mind . I said that I would go to traffic , if he pleased ; that I would renounce my amour , so as to be obedient to him .

sections.label: Section 1.1.85
sections.original: agit grátias mi atque ingenium adlaudat meum ;
sections.translation: He gave me thanks , and praised my good feeling , but failed not to exact my promise ; he built a merchant-ship [*] , and purchased merchandize ; the ship ready , he placed it on board ; besides , to myself with his own hand he paid down a talent of silver ;

sections.label: Section 1.1.90
sections.original: servom una mittit , qui olim puero parvolo
sections.translation: with me he sent a servant , who formerly had been my tutor from the time when I was a little child , to be as though a guardian to me . These things completed , we set sail ; we came to Rhodes , where the merchandize which I had brought I sold to my mind according as I wished ;

sections.label: Section 1.1.95
sections.original: lucrum ingens facio praeterquam mihi meus pater
sections.translation: I made great profits , beyond the estimate of the merchandize which my father had given me ; and so I made a large sum . But while in the harbour I was walking there , a certain stranger recognized me , and invited me to dinner . I went , and took my place at table , being merrily and handsomely entertained .

sections.label: Section 1.1.100
sections.original: discubitum noctu ut imus , ecce ad me advenit
sections.translation: When at night we went to rest , behold , a female came to me , than whom not another female is there more charming . That night , by order of my entertainer , did she pass with me ; consider your own selves , how very much he gratified me . Next day , I went to my host ; I begged him to sell her to me ;

sections.label: Section 1.1.105
sections.original: dico eius pro méritis gratum me et munem fore .
sections.translation: I said that for his kindnesses I should ever be grateful and obliged . What need is there of talking ? I bought her , and yesterday I brought her hither . I don ’ t wish my father to come to know I ’ ve brought her . For the present , I ’ ve left her and a servant in the harbour on board the ship . But why do I see my servant running hither from the harbour ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.110
sections.original: quem navi abire vetui ? timeo quid siet .
sections.translation: whom I forbade to leave the ship ? I dread what the reason may be .

sections.label: Section 1.2.111
sections.original: Ex súmmis opibus víribusque usque éxperire , nítere ,
sections.translation: With your utmost power and might always try and endeavour that your younger master [*] may by your aid be preserved . Come then , Acanthio , away with weariness from you ; take care and be on your guard against sloth . At the same time put an end to this panting ; troth , I can hardly fetch my breath ;

sections.label: Section 1.2.115
sections.original: simul autem plenis semitis qui adversum eunt : aspellito ,
sections.translation: at the same time , too , drive right full against all those persons who come in the way , shove them aside , and push them into the road . This custom here is a very bad one ; no one thinks it proper for him to give way to one who is running and in haste ; and thus three things must be done at the same moment , when you have commenced upon but one ; you must both run and fight , and squabble as well , upon the road .

sections.label: Section 1.2.120
sections.original: Quid illúc est quod ille tam expedite exquirit cursuram sibi ?
sections.translation: What ’ s the reason of this , that he ’ s requiring speed for himself at a rate so rapid ? I have some anxiety , what the business is , or what news he brings .

sections.label: Section 1.2.121b
sections.original: Nugas ago .
sections.translation: I ’ m trifling about it . The more I stop , the greater the risk that ’ s run .

sections.label: Section 1.2.123
sections.original: Mali nescio quid nuntiat .
sections.translation: He brings news of some misfortune , I know not what .

sections.label: Section 1.2.123b
sections.original: Genua hunc cursorem deserunt ;
sections.translation: His knees are failing this runner . I ’ m undone , my spleen is in rebellion [*] , it ’ s taking possession of my breast .

sections.label: Section 1.2.125
sections.original: perii , animam nequeo vertere , nimis nihili tibicen siem .
sections.translation: I ’ m done up , I can ’ t draw my breath . A very worthless piper should I be .

sections.label: Section 1.2.126
sections.original: At tu edepol sume laciniam atque absterge sudorem tibi .
sections.translation: But , i ’ faith , do take the skirt of your coat [*] , and wipe the sweat from off you .

sections.label: Section 1.2.127
sections.original: numquam edepol omnes balineae mi hanc lassitudinem eximent .
sections.translation: I ’ faith , not all the baths will ever remove this lassitude from me . Am I to say that my master Charinus is at home or abroad ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.128b
sections.original: Ego animi pendeo .
sections.translation: I ’ m doubtful in my mind what the matter is ; I ’ d like for myself to learn of him , that I may become acquainted with it .

sections.label: Section 1.2.130
sections.original: Át etiam asto ? at etiam cesso foribus facere hisce assulas ?
sections.translation: But why still standing here ? Why still hesitating to make splinters of this door ? ( Knocks at the door of DEMIPHO ’ S house , and calls .) Open the door , some one . Where ’ s my master , Charinus ? Is he at home or abroad ? Does any one think fit to come to the door ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.132b
sections.original: Ecce me ,
sections.translation: Why , here am I , whom you ’ re looking for , Acanthio .

sections.label: Section 1.2.133b
sections.original: Nusquamst disciplina ignavior .
sections.translation: There is nowhere a more lazy management than in his house .

sections.label: Section 1.2.134
sections.original: Quae té malae res ágitant ?
sections.translation: What matter is afflicting you so terribly ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.134b
sections.original: Multae , ere , te átque me .
sections.translation: Many , master , both yourself and me .

sections.label: Section 1.2.135
sections.original: Quid ést negoti ?
sections.translation: What ’ s the matter ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.135b
sections.original: Périimus .
sections.translation: We are undone .

sections.label: Section 1.2.135c
sections.original: Principium id inimicis dato .
sections.translation: That beginning do you present unto our foes .

sections.label: Section 1.2.136
sections.original: At tibi sortito id optigit .
sections.translation: But your own self it has befallen , as fate would have it .

sections.label: Section 1.2.137
sections.original: Loquere íd negoti quidquid est .
sections.translation: Tell me this matter , whatever it is .

sections.label: Section 1.2.137b
sections.original: Placide , volo adquiescere .
sections.translation: Quietly — I want to take a rest .

sections.label: Section 1.2.138
sections.original: tua causa rupi ramites , iam dudum sputo sanguinem .
sections.translation: For your sake , I ’ ve burst the veins of my lungs ; I ’ m spitting blood already .

sections.label: Section 1.2.139
sections.original: Resinam ex melle Aegyptiam vorato , salvom feceris .
sections.translation: Swallow Aegyptian resin with honey ; you ’ ll make it all right .

sections.label: Section 1.2.140
sections.original: At edepol tu calidam picem bibito , aegritudo abscesserit .
sections.translation: Then , i ’ faith , do you drink hot pitch [*] ; then your troubles will vanish .

sections.label: Section 1.2.141
sections.original: Hóminem ego iracundiorem quam te novi neminem .
sections.translation: I know no one a more tetchy fellow than yourself .

sections.label: Section 1.2.142
sections.original: At ego maledicentiorem quam te novi neminem .
sections.translation: And I know no one more abusive than yourself

sections.label: Section 1.2.143
sections.original: Sin saluti quod tibi esse censeo , id consuadeo ?
sections.translation: But what if I ’ m persuading you to that which I take to be for your benefit ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.144
sections.original: Apage istiusmodi salutem , cum cruciatu quae advenit .
sections.translation: Away with benefit of that sort , that ’ s accompanied with pain .

sections.label: Section 1.2.145
sections.original: Dic mihi , an boni quid usquamst , quod quisquam uti possiet
sections.translation: Tell me , is there any good at all that any one can enjoy entirely without evil ; or where you mustn ’ t endure labour when you wish to enjoy it ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.147
sections.original: Nescio ego istaec : philosophari numquam didici neque scio .
sections.translation: I don ’ t understand these things ; I never learnt to philosophize , and don ’ t know how . I don ’ t want any good to be given me , to which evil is an accompaniment .

sections.label: Section 1.2.149
sections.original: Cedo tuam mihi dexteram , agedum , Acanthio .
sections.translation: Come now , Acanthio , give me your right hand .

sections.label: Section 1.2.149b
sections.original: Em dabitur , tene .
sections.translation: It shall be given ; there then , take it .

sections.label: Section 1.2.150
sections.original: Vin tu te mihi obsequentem esse an nevis ?
sections.translation: Do you intend yourself to be obedient to me , or don ’ t you intend it ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.150b
sections.original: Opera licet
sections.translation: You may judge by experience , as I ’ ve ruptured myself with running for your sake , in order that what I knew , you might have the means of knowing directly .

sections.label: Section 1.2.152b
sections.original: Liberum
sections.translation: I ’ ll make you a free man within a few months .

sections.label: Section 1.2.153b
sections.original: Palpo percutis .
sections.translation: You are smoothing me down .

sections.label: Section 1.2.154_155
sections.original: Egon ausim tibi usquam quicquam facinus falsum proloqui ?
sections.translation: What , should I presume ever to make mention of an untrue thing to you ? On the contrary , before I said so , you knew already whether I intend to utter an untruth .
